coreJobStatement: Deliver accredited higher education programs and credentials to enrolled students within a safe, legally compliant, and research-capable campus environment
productJobStatement: Help Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management to secure emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while maintaining fire-code-compliant single-motion escape capability and reporting door status events to centralized monitoring — across distributed campus buildings with aging, heterogeneous infrastructure
lPath: L5.h1.2 — Emergency Exit Alarm Guards → L6-H1 — Campus Safety, Security & Emergency Management

segments.name: S1 — Legacy building stock
segments.characteristics: Buildings >40 years old; no existing wired alarm infrastructure; surface-mount only viable path. Dominant in US public universities; >75% of campus facilities 30-40+ years old.
segments.targetabilityReason: Wired alternatives not viable; wireless retrofit is the only path
segments.circumstance: Building constructed >40 years ago; no existing wired alarm infrastructure; surface-mount install is the only viable path

segments.name: S2 — Research labs and controlled-access facilities
segments.characteristics: High-value equipment, hazardous materials, or export-controlled research; after-hours unauthorized entry risk elevated. R1/R2 research universities; EAR/ITAR-controlled zones.
segments.circumstance: High-value equipment, hazardous materials, or export-controlled research; after-hours unauthorized entry risk elevated
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — higher sensitivity, stricter audit requirements

segments.name: S3 — Residential dormitories
segments.characteristics: 24/7 student occupancy; elopement and unauthorized external entry risk; building occupied when security staffing is minimal.
segments.targetabilityReason: 24/7 monitoring requirement; battery life critical at high-use exits
segments.circumstance: 24/7 student occupancy; elopement and unauthorized external entry risk; building occupied when security staffing is minimal
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — 24/7 monitoring requirement; battery life critical at high-use exits

segments.name: S4 — High-occupancy assembly spaces
segments.characteristics: Lecture halls, event centers, performance venues with variable, large-scale occupancy; emergency exits critical under peak load.
segments.targetabilityReason: False alarm cost high during public events
segments.circumstance: Lecture halls, event centers, performance venues with variable, large-scale occupancy; emergency exits critical under peak load
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — false alarm cost high during public events

segments.name: S5 — Open-access public buildings
segments.characteristics: Libraries, student unions, visitor centers open to the general public; unauthorized entry difficult to detect via credential-based access alone.
segments.targetabilityReason: Perimeter security less controlled; more frequent door events
segments.circumstance: Libraries, student unions, visitor centers open to the general public; unauthorized entry difficult to detect via credential-based access alone
segments.alternativesDiffer: Yes — perimeter security less controlled; more frequent door events

alternatives.name: GfS Tuerwachter (mechanical + IoT alarm guard) — spring-loaded handle guard with EasyWave radio
alternatives.tradeoffs: EasyWave protocol lock-in — not interoperable with campus PSIM natively; 30 m wireless range requires repeaters in large buildings; battery replacement maintenance cycle; surface-mount visible protrusion on door handle

alternatives.name: Electronic Exit Control Systems (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) — mains-powered electronic exit control
alternatives.tradeoffs: Requires mains power at door — major construction in legacy buildings; high per-door installation cost; licensed contractor required; single point of failure if power-dependent alarm

alternatives.name: Basic mechanical door prop alarms (Detex EC-100, Seco-Larm) — contact alarm, no monitoring
alternatives.tradeoffs: Local alarm only — no centralized monitoring; no Clery Act-compliant documentation; no battery status visibility; no IoT; security officer must be on-site to hear alarm

alternatives.name: Campus access control with alarmed exit hardware (ASSA ABLOY Aperio, Allegion Von Duprin) — credential-based control with alarmed release
alternatives.tradeoffs: Requires full IT provisioning and credential management; high system complexity; wired infrastructure; designed for access control, not standalone exit alarm monitoring; high integration cost for legacy buildings

alternatives.name: Campus police patrol + manual inspection rounds
alternatives.tradeoffs: 2-4 FTEs for full 24/7 campus coverage; no real-time detection; manual compliance logs — error-prone; patrol frequency gaps at night; does not meet continuous monitoring expectations of fire marshals or insurance underwriters

alternatives.name: Do nothing / accept non-compliance
alternatives.tradeoffs: Zero cost. Clery Act violation risk; NFPA/state fire marshal fine exposure (>$10K/violation); insurance liability exposure; no monitoring; reputational risk at accreditation review

jobSteps.description: Identify which campus exit doors require alarm guard coverage per NFPA 101, IBC Chapter 10, and campus security policy
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Scoping errors lead to gaps in mandated coverage
jobSteps.rawStatement: Identify which campus exits require coverage; fire code specifications; campus security policy
jobSteps.rationale: Scoping errors lead to gaps in mandated coverage

jobSteps.description: Assess door hardware types, building RF environment, and distance from doors to wireless gateway
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: RF coverage and door compatibility determine install complexity
jobSteps.rawStatement: Assess door hardware types, building RF environment, distance to gateway
jobSteps.rationale: RF coverage and door compatibility determine install complexity

jobSteps.description: Install and configure alarm guards; provision wireless receivers; establish campus monitoring dashboard
jobSteps.rawStatement: Install and configure alarm guards; provision receivers; establish monitoring dashboard

jobSteps.description: Verify device functionality, wireless transmission, alarm trigger, and key override before go-live
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Silent commissioning failures are the main deployment risk
jobSteps.rawStatement: Verify device functionality, wireless connectivity, alarm trigger, key override
jobSteps.rationale: Silent commissioning failures are the main deployment risk

jobSteps.description: Alarm guards continuously secure exit doors; alarm triggers on unauthorized activation
jobSteps.rawStatement: Devices actively guard doors; alarm triggers on unauthorized activation

jobSteps.description: Centralized dashboard aggregates door status, alarm events, and battery levels across all campus buildings
jobSteps.rawStatement: Dashboard aggregates door status, alarms, battery levels across campus

jobSteps.description: Replace batteries, respond to alarm events, add/remove devices as campus footprint changes
jobSteps.rawStatement: Battery replacement; device add/remove; configuration updates

jobSteps.description: Compile compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report
jobSteps.sensorDependencyRationale: Compliance documentation = key differentiator vs. basic alarms
jobSteps.rawStatement: Compile compliance records for fire inspections, Clery Act reporting, insurance
jobSteps.rationale: Compliance documentation = key differentiator vs. basic alarms

stakeholders.who: Campus Security Officer / Facility Security Manager — monitors dashboard, responds to alerts, manages devices
stakeholders.pyramidLevels: P1 ALL + P2 + P3 + P4 + P5

stakeholders.who: VP Facilities / Chief Security Officer — accountable for Clery Act compliance, fire inspections, insurance
stakeholders.pyramidLevels: P1 own + P2 + P4 ALL + P5 rarely

stakeholders.who: Installer / Facilities Maintenance Tech — installs, commissions, replaces batteries
Facilities Director / Procurement Officer — approves budget, signs contracts
Fire Marshal / State Fire Inspector / Insurance Auditor / Campus Safety Committee

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete emergency exit coverage mapping that leaves fire-code-mandated campus exits unmonitored after initial deployment
Minimize the effort required to assess door hardware compatibility and wireless signal reach across heterogeneous campus building stock before device selection
Minimize the likelihood of RF dead zones in concrete campus buildings causing emergency exit devices to transmit outside effective receiver range
Minimize installation labor-hours required to deploy exit monitoring devices across a multi-building campus without licensed electrical contractor involvement
Minimize variability in alarm guard installation quality across lever-handle, panic-bar, and glass-frame door configurations during a campus-wide rollout
Minimize the likelihood of deploying exit alarm devices with undetected wireless connectivity failures that are not discovered until after campus go-live
Minimize the effort required to verify wireless transmission integrity between each newly installed device and the central receiver before commissioning sign-off
Minimize the likelihood of alarm guard mechanical failure in high-traffic campus exit environments causing an unguarded door state
Minimize the likelihood of false alarm triggers caused by accidental door contact during normal building operations disrupting campus activities
Minimize the time to detect and route an unauthorized emergency exit opening event to the campus security operations center
Minimize the number of door events failing to reach the central monitoring dashboard due to RF signal attenuation in reinforced-concrete campus buildings
Minimize variability in battery depletion rates across devices deployed in buildings with different door-use frequencies and ambient temperatures
Minimize the time between a campus emergency exit device losing wireless connectivity and the security operations center detecting the monitoring gap
Increase the ability to monitor all campus emergency exit devices from a single security dashboard without requiring building-specific logins or separate hardware systems
Minimize the time required to replace an exit alarm guard battery without triggering the alarm horn or temporarily disabling the door's fire-code escape path
Minimize the time to restore full exit alarm and wireless monitoring function after a device fault or battery exhaustion event
Minimize the labor required to compile emergency exit compliance documentation for annual fire safety inspections and Clery Act Annual Security Report filings
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act compliance gaps due to missing door event records from campus exit devices that experienced intermittent wireless outages
Minimize the risk of regulatory fine exposure from undocumented emergency exit non-compliance events discovered during unannounced NFPA or state fire marshal audits
Increase the ability to scale emergency exit monitoring incrementally across a campus building-by-building without system-wide infrastructure changes or full re-commissioning
Minimize the effort required to evaluate exit monitoring vendor interoperability with existing campus PSIM and Building Management Systems before procurement commitment
Minimize the likelihood of Clery Act Annual Security Report inaccuracies caused by manual compilation of door-event records not captured in a central digital log
Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit non-compliance being discovered during an unannounced fire marshal visit rather than through the institution's own continuous monitoring
Increase the ability to generate audit-ready emergency exit compliance reports demonstrating date-stamped 100% door-event coverage for the prior 12 months, on demand within minutes
Minimize the likelihood of undetected after-hours emergency exit breaches remaining unresolved until the next morning's security review
Increase the ability to provide fire marshals with continuous, remotely accessible exit-status records that eliminate the need for 100% on-site physical inspection of every door
